items.title: Osteopenia is silently weakening bones in millions of people
items.summary: Osteopenia is a common but often overlooked condition that causes bones to become less dense and more fragile. Because it develops silently, many people only discover they have it after a fracture or bone scan. Aging, menopause, poor diet, and inactivity can all contribute to bone loss. Fortunately,

items.title: Shorter front-leg strides can be an early warning sign of dementia in senior dogs
items.summary: Scientists have shown that the stride length of the front legs (but not the hind legs) of senior and geriatric dogs decreases as their cognitive performance worsens. In contrast, chronological age itself was a poor predictor of stride length. A similar reduction in step length has been well document

items.title: A Turquoise Tint for the Black Sea
items.summary: Phytoplankton added a milky blue hue to the waters of the Black Sea and nearby waterways in spring and summer 2026.

items.title: A NASA satellite caught a giant tsunami doing something no one expected
items.summary: A Pacific-wide tsunami triggered by a magnitude 8.8 Kamchatka earthquake gave scientists their first detailed satellite view of a major tsunami in motion. The observations revealed unexpected wave behavior and helped uncover a larger earthquake rupture than earlier models predicted.

items.title: Precious drops: Spray helps crop farmers facing declining rainfall
items.summary: A spray made from a biodegradable polymer capable of capturing and redirecting water to crop seeds could be the key to drought-proofing Western Australian farms.

items.title: Designer proteins unlock near-infrared and SWIR glow for deeper tissue imaging
items.summary: Researchers at the National Center for Tumor Diseases (NCT/UCC) in Dresden, including Oliver Bruns and Dr. Bernardo Arús, are participating in an international study that has, for the first time, developed novel proteins for near-infrared (NIR) and short-wave infrared imaging (SWIR). The research wa

items.title: Ancient stories inform modern understanding of volcanic eruptions
items.summary: A new international study has found that Indigenous oral traditions, some thousands of years old, hold valuable and often overlooked insights into volcanic eruptions—offering important lessons for modern disaster preparedness. The research, published in the journal Volcanica, was led by Museums Vict

items.title: 'Fingerprints' of black hole's event horizon detected for first time
items.summary: Scientists have detected the "fingerprints" of a black hole's event horizon—the boundary from which nothing can escape—for the first time, according to research published Wednesday.

items.title: 'Super-puff' planets lighter than candy floss discovered by international team
items.summary: An international collaboration has discovered two of the lowest-density giant planets ever detected: rare "super-puff" planets with densities lower than candy floss. The study—led by the University of Oxford, in collaboration with Université Côte d'Azur/Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur and t

items.title: NASA’s TESS Mission Reveals the “Puffiest” Planets Ever Found
items.summary: NASA has revealed two new “super-puff” planets, giant worlds so light that their density is comparable to cotton candy. Scientists calculate that these Jupiter-sized planets are the “puffiest” worlds ever found.

items.title: Pesticide widespread in blood of French Indies adults: Study
items.summary: Eight in 10 adults in the French West Indies have a toxic pesticide in their blood decades after banana growers stopped using it, a study said Wednesday.

items.title: Did gravitational tides cause Earth's extinctions?
items.summary: Life on Earth took a long evolutionary journey that eventually created us, the purportedly intelligent species that dominates the planet. But there was no grand plan or design, only happenstance, nature and luck. Life on Earth suffered multiple extinctions, but got up, dusted itself off and continue

items.title: Laser experiments push helium to record shock pressures
items.summary: Deep inside gas giants like Jupiter and Saturn, hydrogen and helium coexist under pressures millions of times greater than Earth's atmosphere. Under those conditions, helium may separate from hydrogen and influence a planet's internal heat flow, structure and magnetic field. Understanding

items.title: How longer exciton lifetimes could ease efficiency trade-off in organic solar cells
items.summary: Although the efficiency of organic solar cells has now risen to more than 20%, there are physical limits that make it difficult to further increase their performance. A research team from Linköping University in Sweden, the University of Potsdam, the Paul-Drude-Institut in Berlin and other collabora

items.title: Mathematical modeling helps advance use of magnetic particles in targeted drug-delivery systems
items.summary: A Florida State University computational scientist is paving the way for future medical breakthroughs by developing mathematical models and simulations to predict the behavior of a unique drug-delivery method, which aims to deploy treatments directly to targeted sites in the body.

items.title: Drifting tuna gear creates risks for wildlife in protected marine areas
items.summary: An international study co-authored by a University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa researcher has found that drifting devices used by the global tuna fishing industry are entering marine protected areas around the world, creating potential risks for wildlife and sensitive ocean habitats.

items.title: Web archive lets you easily search millions of government documents
items.summary: At the end of every presidential term, the End of Term Web Archive preserves that administration's web presence as a vast trove of documents and webpages. The archive began in 2008, with George W. Bush's second term, and runs through 2024, collecting images, text, graphs, redacted pages an

items.title: A magnetic field that kills superconductivity can also bring it back
items.summary: Magnetic fields are generally known to destroy superconductivity in a material. However, in exceptional cases, they can lead to what is known as "re-entrant superconductivity"—where superconductivity disappears as expected, but then unexpectedly returns when the magnetic field is increased further.

items.title: Euclid mission view of Milky Way's heart previews upcoming survey by NASA's Roman
items.summary: A new look at the heart of our Milky Way galaxy by Euclid, an ESA (European Space Agency) mission with NASA contributions, overlaps with a region scientists will observe with NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, launching later this summer. This sneak peek gives astronomers a major jumpsta

items.title: X-ray snapshots reveal how viral shells change shape as they dry out
items.summary: When viruses travel through the air in tiny droplets, they can quickly start to dry out. Yet many viruses remain infectious after rehydration—something that is still not fully understood. Now, an international team of researchers has directly observed at the European XFEL how the protein shells of v

items.title: Free love in communes? Not quite, says researcher
items.summary: The myth of the sexually liberated life of communes is persistent, even though research shows a different reality. According to postdoctoral researcher Anna Heinonen of the University of Eastern Finland, the idea that roommates would have very free sexual relationships with each other is a strange c

items.title: What happens when environmental change outpaces life's ability to adapt?
items.summary: When an animal's environment changes faster than the animal can adapt, its chances of survival can flatline. The same is true for populations and even entire species. Now, scientists at MIT and the University of Leicester have found that this connection between evolutionary adaptation and the p

items.title: A nanotrap for HIV: Liposomes repurposed to trigger immune response
items.summary: Medical advancements over the last several decades have made great strides in the treatment of HIV. Pharmaceutical treatments are able to contain and reduce a patient's viral load to the point where it is nearly undetectable. But a cure remains frustratingly elusive due to the virus's abil

items.title: Room-temperature laser hits record stability with 68-cm optical cavity
items.summary: Scientists at NPL have demonstrated the best-reported laser frequency stability achieved with an optical reference cavity operating at room temperature, marking a major advance in ultrastable laser technology. The team's results have been published in Optica.

items.title: NASA at the Ion: Orion Lessons from Artemis II Shape NASA’s Moon to Mars Path
items.summary: Seven weeks after the Orion spacecraft returned four astronauts from humanity’s first crewed journey around the Moon since Apollo, Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager Branelle Rodriguez reflected on the mission’s achievements and how it is shaping

items.title: La NASA compartirá los últimos avances del programa Base Lunar
items.summary: Lea esta nota de prensa en inglés aquí. El administrador de la NASA, Jared Isaacman, ofrecerá una conversación virtual el martes 30 de junio a las 2:30 p.m. EDT (hora del este) para compartir las novedades más recientes sobre los planes de la agencia para construir una base en la superficie de la Lu

items.title: NASA to Share Latest Moon Base Mission Progress
items.summary: NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman will host a virtual conversation at 2:30 p.m. EDT, Tuesday, June 30, to share updates to NASA’s plans to build a Moon Base on the lunar surface. Administrator Isaacman and Carlos García-Galán, Moon Base program manager, will discuss the next set of awards for new lu

items.title: Tri sestre iz Brazila zajedno imaju 316 godina. Znanstvenici proučavaju njihov DNK
items.summary: TRI BRAZILSKE sestre, s ukupno 316 godina, najstarije su na svijetu. Znanstvenici proučavaju njihov DNK kako bi otkrili genetske tajne dugovječnosti i otpornosti na bolesti starenja.

items.title: Oysters used as living labs reveal unexpected stability in ocean virus populations
items.summary: Oysters filter seawater for food. In the process, they concentrate a wide variety of microorganisms from their environment—including bacteria and viruses—into a tiny space.

items.title: Fair Workweek laws improve work schedules without cutting pay or benefits, according to research
items.summary: A study examining Fair Workweek laws across five major U.S. jurisdictions finds that labor regulations have made work schedules more predictable for service-sector workers, without triggering wage cuts or benefit reductions. Published in Science Advances, the research titled "Fair Workweek Laws in t

items.title: Images: Perseverance reaches 'marathon' milestone on Mars
items.summary: NASA's Perseverance rover appears as a green speck on the Martian surface on June 13, 2026, a day before the robotic explorer marked a distance milestone, having traveled a full marathon (26.2 miles, or 42.195 kilometers) on the Red Planet. Perseverance reached that distance after five years an

items.title: Are algorithms unfairly screening out immigrant job applications?
items.summary: Canada's new artificial intelligence strategy, AI for All, presents an ambitious vision for the country's future. Artificial intelligence, the federal government argues, can boost productivity, strengthen competitiveness and create opportunity across the economy.

items.title: Preserving wooden heritage in the Arctic as thaw, rot and tourism converge
items.summary: Historic wooden structures across Svalbard are crumbling under the combined weight of climate change and human activity. Longer, warmer, and wetter seasons fuel wood-decaying fungi, while tourism adds physical wear to sites never built to last. The ArcticAlpineDecay project has provided a crucial ba

items.title: Roman Telescope Comes to Kennedy
items.summary: In this June 21, 2026, photo, NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope arrives at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida aboard NASA’s Pegasus barge. After offloading and transportation to the spaceport’s Payload Hazardous Servicing Facility, Roman will undergo processing ahead of launch, targ

items.title: Scientists design 'tunable' biomolecules to probe how sugars behave
items.summary: Sugars are not just a source of energy—they also play a crucial role in how cells communicate, how proteins interact and how materials behave in medicine and industry. But studying these processes is challenging because sugar molecules are structurally complex and difficult to control.

items.title: How 'catchy' music is driven by rhythmic patterns
items.summary: Puerto Rican icon Bad Bunny, a superstar rapper, has recently risen to global prominence, as demonstrated by the 2026 Super Bowl halftime show.

items.title: Ljudski DNK star 2000 godina pronađen na zidovima špilja u Španjolskoj
items.summary: ZNANSTVENICI su na zidovima špilja u Španjolskoj i Portugalu otkrili ljudski DNK star preko 2000 godina. Ovo otkriće otvara nove mogućnosti za proučavanje prapovijesnih populacija.

items.title: A new quantum computer sets a high watermark for accuracy. Are we on the verge of a big breakthrough?
items.summary: In a laboratory in Broomfield, Colorado, 98 atoms are suspended in midair, held in place by electric fields and cooled to temperatures close to absolute zero.

items.title: 'Collapsible scissored surfaces' complete trilogy of metamaterial design principles
items.summary: Over the past decade, Professor L. Mahadevan's Soft Math Lab at the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) has helped establish how the ancient Japanese paper arts of folding or cutting can be used to inversely design structures that transform dramatically in

items.title: Fiber-optic cables detect silent whales off Svalbard by tracking pressure waves
items.summary: A 100-year-old equation and a fiber-optic cable off the coast of Svalbard led researchers to discover they could detect swimming whales—even if they were completely silent. The discovery broadens the tools biologists could someday use to detect and monitor these marine giants.

items.title: AI spots landslide risks near power towers before failures, tests show
items.summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) can identify landslides and other geological changes that threaten electricity transmission towers, potentially helping operators intervene before infrastructure fails, according to research in the International Journal of Power and Energy Conversion.

items.title: NASA’s HiRISE Captures Perseverance Marking a Milestone on Mars
items.summary: Description NASA’s Perseverance rover appears as a green speck on the Martian surface on June 13, 2026, a day before the robotic explorer marked a distance milestone, having traveled a full marathon (26.2 miles, or 42.195 kilometers) on the Red Planet. Perseverance reached that distance after five y

items.title: Euclid View of Milky Way Heart Previews Core Survey by NASA’s Roman
items.summary: A new look at the heart of our Milky Way galaxy by Euclid, an ESA (European Space Agency) mission with NASA contributions, overlaps with a region scientists will observe with NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope, launching later this summer. This sneak peek gives astronomers a major jumpstart on

items.title: Mathematicians unleash multifold speed boost for supercomputer simulations of molecules
items.summary: More than 20% of the workload on the world's 500 fastest supercomputers is spent simulating how atoms and molecules move—with applications ranging from material design to identifying drug interactions to understanding protein folding.

items.title: Expert studies emergence of identity-based labor organizing
items.summary: In the post-COVID-19 era, worker unionization campaigns have increasingly been organized by groups who feel stung by virtue signaling from corporations espousing progressive values, especially those pertaining to LGBTQIA+ issues.

items.title: The Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool is treated with nanobubbles. What are they and how do they work?
items.summary: As the United States approaches its 250th birthday celebrations on July 4, Washington, DC's monuments, statues and fountains are being prepared to put on a show.

items.title: Human DNA can survive on cave walls for thousands of years, opening new window into prehistory
items.summary: For the first time, scientists have shown that ancient human DNA can survive for thousands of years on cave walls, opening new ways to study prehistoric human activity. This interdisciplinary study was conducted within the framework of the First Art project, which is led by researchers from Spain an

items.title: Heavy rain may be driving tire pollution into Florida waterways
items.summary: Florida International University scientists have, for the first time, detected a toxic tire-derived chemical in Florida waterways and developed a new testing method that makes it easier to find and monitor the pollutant at extremely low levels. The study focuses on 6PPD-quinone (6PPD-Q), a toxic che

items.title: Pop song lyrics grew more self-focused in the US and Germany over 50 years, research reveals
items.summary: Over five decades, popular songs in the U.S. and Germany have become more self-focused—as indicated by the use of pronouns such as "I," "me" and "mine"—while no such trend was seen for the most popular songs in Japan and Hong Kong. Marius Golubickis of United Arab Emirates University and colleagues

items.title: Sicily remained a medieval melting pot despite major political and religious upheavals, ancient DNA reveals
items.summary: Sicilian populations have been genetically diverse for many centuries, and they have remained that way even through major regime changes and religious transitions, according to a study published in PLOS One by Aurore Monnereau of the University of York, U.K., and colleagues.

items.title: Decades-long dataset shows which orcas are most at home in Puget Sound
items.summary: Data spanning nearly half a century shows that endangered southern resident killer whales are spending less time in inland waters, whereas their larger cousins, Bigg's killer whales, are increasingly present in Puget Sound.

items.title: Introducing Weather Jiu-Jitsu, a new approach to avert catastrophic weather events
items.summary: In a new perspective paper, Qin Huang of Arizona State University and colleagues propose that the worst damage from extreme weather events could be prevented through Weather Jiu-Jitsu, a theory-based approach to "nudge" weather systems away from harmful trajectories. The paper was published in PLOS

items.title: Machine learning rediscovers equations governing ocean biogeochemistry
items.summary: Climate and ocean models use a series of equations to represent complex natural processes. However, the equations used in these models are often derived from limited observations and a series of assumptions.

items.title: Earth's oldest crater really is more than 3 billion years old, new study confirms
items.summary: In the Pilbara of Western Australia, some of Earth's oldest rocks lie beneath the sky, as they have for billions of years. They are dark, weathered volcanic rocks, close to 3.5 billion years old, cut by veins and stewed by deep time.

items.title: Kako rashladiti sebe i dom usred toplinskog vala? Evo savjeta
items.summary: POSTOJE brojni trikovi kojima se možemo zaštititi od vrućina.

items.title: Znanstvenici možda prvi put detektirali signal s ruba crne rupe
items.summary: ZNANSTVENICI vjeruju da su u gravitacijskom valu iz sudara crnih rupa identificirali 'izravni val', prvi zabilježeni potpis horizonta događaja, što otvara nove načine proučavanja ovih objekata.

items.title: Binary black hole signal probes event horizon region for first time
items.summary: If, in space, no one can hear you scream, it seems that you can actually hear the sound of a crash when two black holes collide. Using the loudest gravitational wave ever heard, two Australian scientists and colleagues have been the first to witness the previously elusive "event horizon" at the actu

items.title: We discovered a new rock type containing garnet inside a meteorite fragment from Mars
items.summary: On Earth, garnet is best known as the fiery red January birthstone—popular in jewelry since the Bronze Age and valued highly by ancient Egyptians.

items.title: Hidden botanical treasures in war-torn Kyiv need global support, study shows
items.summary: One of the world's most significant collections of plant specimens is under threat from the ongoing war in Ukraine, prompting an international call for urgent digitization and global collaboration to preserve an irreplaceable scientific resource.

items.title: Ancient proteins hint at all-female Homo naledi burial site in Rising Star cave system
items.summary: Scientists have extracted and analyzed the first-ever ancient proteins from the fossils of Homo naledi, revealing a potential all-female burial site. The study, published in the journal Cell, raises the possibility that South Africa's famous Rising Star Cave system could represent the first kno

items.title: Language-based screeners may miss kids who struggle to read due to visual-processing issues
items.summary: Reading difficulties, like dyslexia, are common and often affect achievement and outcomes during school and later in life. A new study, published in Current Biology, reports that current methods used to test for reading disabilities in young children may be missing a certain subgroup of children who

items.title: Climate warnings need to be told in tangible ways to prevent disaster
items.summary: England is sweltering under a red heat health alert and could see its hottest June day on record. In North America, football fans and players are suffering, with a quarter of this summer's World Cup matches forecast to be played in dangerous heat.

items.title: Euclid captures 60 million stars in sharpest broad view of Milky Way's core
items.summary: For just one day, our dark universe detective, Euclid, turned its gaze toward the light: the extremely bright inner region of our Milky Way galaxy, known as the galactic bulge. This special request came from astronomers who were after what Euclid does best: capturing huge areas of the sky in crisp d

items.title: Does the Netherlands feed the world? Study challenges a familiar view of Dutch agriculture
items.summary: The Netherlands is a major agricultural exporter. But look beyond euros to land, animal feed, calories and protein, and a different picture emerges. In a study published in Nature Food, researchers at Wageningen University & Research (WUR) conclude that the Dutch contribution to the global food supp

items.title: Geometric anti-spring works near absolute zero, suppressing vibrations below 0.185 hertz
items.summary: Physicists and instrument makers in Leiden have succeeded in optimizing a spring that almost completely filters out vibrations at temperatures near absolute zero. This breakthrough opens the door to a new generation of highly sensitive experiments. The research is published in the journal Measuremen

items.title: Kontracepcijske pilule kod nekih žena potiču prejedanje, pokazuje studija
items.summary: ORALNI kontraceptivi povećavaju vjerojatnost prejedanja kod nekih žena zbog utjecaja na hormone, zaključio je tim istraživača koji je sedam tjedana pratio više od 400 ispitanica za potrebe studije čiji su rezultati objavljeni u stručnom časopisu JAMA Network Open.

items.title: Automated system detects early signs of nanomaterials toxicity
items.summary: Korea Research Institute of Standards and Science has developed a toxicity assessment system that automatically measures and analyzes the heart rate of Daphnia magna. Capable of processing heart rate data from approximately 150 individuals per hour, the system can assess the toxic effects of polluta

items.title: Artificial DNA tiles could deliver drugs and monitor neurons non-disruptively
items.summary: Living cells constantly exchange ions (i.e., charged particles) via the thin barrier that surrounds their interior, known as the outer membrane. Neuroscientists and medical researchers have long been trying to devise effective methods to measure this exchange of ions, which is known to be associated

items.title: Znanstvenici: Ljudi koji vole luk rjeđe razvijaju dijabetes i povišeni tlak
items.summary: NOVA studija otkriva genetsku vezu između sklonosti luku i manjeg rizika od dijabetesa tipa 2 i visokog tlaka. Pristup temeljen na genima za okus i miris obećava bolja nutricionistička istraživanja.

items.title: New giant wormlion fly species identified on the southern slopes of the Himalayas
items.summary: An enigmatic new species of wormlion fly, whose larvae construct clever pitfall traps to capture prey, has been revealed in a study led by researchers at Dali University in China.

items.title: More people today have a stronger belief in their own ability to shape their lives
items.summary: People living in Germany have more confidence in themselves today than 20 years ago. They have more faith in their ability to influence their own lives and key life events. This has now been shown in a long-term study conducted by Dr. Theresa M. Entringer from the University of Greifswald and collea

items.title: Why female guppies prefer rare males and how this might shape evolution
items.summary: When it comes to choosing a partner, some species prefer males that stand out from the crowd. Evolutionary biologists call the resulting process negative frequency-dependent selection. It means that a male has a huge mating advantage when his appearance is rare but loses that advantage if his look b

items.title: Teaching with and about GenAI in classrooms
items.summary: It is important that teachers and students are aware of and understand the major shortcomings of technology, state Norwegian researchers who have studied the use of AI in the classroom.

items.title: Asteroid zooming past Earth on Saturday visible to stargazers
items.summary: A large asteroid that will zoom harmlessly past Earth on Saturday will be visible to stargazers using a small telescope or large binoculars, the European Space Agency announced Wednesday.

items.title: By making key signaling molecules called β-arrestins into druggable targets, scientists crack long-standing challenge
items.summary: To function normally, nearly every cell in the human body relies on G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) to receive and send signals. That's why GPCRs are targeted by roughly one-third of all FDA-approved drugs.

items.title: Surprising diversity found among Europe's last Neanderthals
items.summary: A new study published in Nature provides the most detailed picture to date of Neanderthal diversity in Western Europe shortly before their extinction.

items.title: First complete map of world's seagrass offers warnings and hope for conservation
items.summary: It's time we gave seagrass the credit it's due. This hero of a plant protects coastlines, stores vast amounts of carbon and supports ecosystems that people and wildlife depend on. But we don't often hear about it when it comes to ocean conservation. That's partly because we knew

items.title: Turning low-value diamond dust into high-performance quantum materials
items.summary: Diamonds have long been coveted for their beauty. Their dazzling color and clarity make them perfect candidates for luxury jewelry. However, it's their other unique characteristics, including their hardness, thermal conductivity and chemical resistance, that make diamonds suitable for various a

items.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
items.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe an

items.title: Quantum squeezing sidesteps the limits on mechanical transducers
items.summary: From detecting the ripples of colliding black holes to imaging individual chemical bonds, mechanical transducers have repeatedly transformed our understanding of the universe. So far, however, the sensitivity of these devices has been intrinsically limited by the laws of quantum mechanics itself.

items.title: Axolotl-inspired skin matrix may help heal wounds with less scarring
items.summary: Researchers in Taiwan have developed a cell-free extracellular matrix material from axolotl skin that helped mouse burn wounds close faster and show signs of reduced fibrotic scarring. The findings suggest that one of nature's most remarkable regenerators may inspire future strategies for burns

items.title: Scientists catch classical space-time crystals moving like Majorana quasiparticles
items.summary: A research team from Hiroshima University, the University of Colorado, and other collaborators have demonstrated that space-time crystals—exotic structures that, under external drive, loop endlessly through both space and time—can be created using everyday liquid-crystal materials.

items.title: Voluntary corporate climate goals are viewed favorably by investors, researchers discover
items.summary: As companies face increasing pressure to address climate change, many are choosing to publicly announce voluntary carbon-elimination goals.

items.title: Sugar-coated nanoparticles show promise for treating most aggressive form of brain cancer
items.summary: Researchers at Oregon State University have potentially found a new way to treat the most aggressive form of brain cancer, glioblastoma, whose two-year survival rate is less than 30%.

items.title: Five years of aerosol remote sensing in Mindelo—a milestone in atmospheric research in the Atlantic
items.summary: For five years now, a distinctive green laser beam has been shining at night up to 30 kilometers (19 miles) above the harbor of the island's capital. It forms part of a high-energy lidar with which the Leibniz Institute for Atmospheric Research (TROPOS) operates continuous aerosol and cloud mea

items.title: From virtue to vice: How the morality of popular music lyrics has changed since the 1960s
items.summary: Popular music may be reflecting a growing culture of vices, according to new research from the Center for Digital Music at Queen Mary University of London. The analysis of musical evolution found that song lyrics have become increasingly negative over the past six decades, with declining references

items.title: Career Spotlight: Electrician (Ages 14-18)
items.summary: What does an electrician do? Electricity powers the world, and electricians are the ones who get it where it needs to go. An electrician is an expert who is trained to make sure electrical systems and equipment are installed safely and working correctly. Electricians are involved in a variety of sys

items.title: Scientists discover ancient brain cells that help block distractions
items.summary: Scientists have discovered a tiny group of neurons in an ancient brain region that acts like a built-in focus filter, helping the brain ignore distractions and zero in on what matters most. When researchers temporarily switched off these neurons in mice, the animals became unusually distractible—sim

items.title: Hot Jupiter endures star-powered barbecue
items.summary: You're the grillmaster at the annual family Fourth of July barbecue, and you're sweating bullets standing over the grill in the sweltering summer heat. You're trying to stay cool by pressing a cold beer can to your forehead, but to no avail. You can't go inside because, once agai

items.title: Do hyenas eat livestock and rhinos? Behavioral biologists investigate a surprisingly charming population
items.summary: For conservation and the management of human–wildlife conflicts, it is of great interest to know which species are eaten by carnivores. Scientists from the Ngorongoro Hyena Project at the Leibniz Institute for Zoo and Wildlife Research (Leibniz-IZW) analyzed this dynamic for spotted hyenas in the Ng

items.title: Scientists discover hidden “footprints of death” that may help viruses spread
items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising new twist in what happens when cells die. As dying cells break apart, they leave behind tiny “footprints of death” packed with newly discovered particles that help guide the immune system to clean up the remains. But researchers found that influenza viruses can

items.title: Interlayer self-doping could unlock room-temperature multiferroics in atom-thin materials
items.summary: Multiferroics are materials that exhibit more than one prominent "ferroic" property, such as ferromagnetism and ferroelectricity. One of their most advantageous features is that they allow engineers to control their magnetic states with electric fields or vice versa, due to an effect known as magnet

items.title: Hospital workers' phones carry deadly superbugs
items.summary: The largest study of its kind has found hospital workers' phones are carrying the same superbugs that kill millions of people worldwide each year. An international team of researchers, including several from Bond University, analyzed DNA samples from 95 mobile phones belonging to health care wo

items.title: Why South Africa’s leopards shrank to half their normal size
items.summary: A hidden population of South African leopards has revealed a remarkable evolutionary story. Researchers analyzing entire leopard genomes discovered that the Cape Floristic Region’s leopards are not only much smaller than most African leopards, but also genetically distinct after being isolated for r

items.title: Buyer beware: Your sustainable shopping choices may not be as green as they look
items.summary: New research reveals that many "green" claims are almost impossible to verify, often masking the full truth. University of Technology (UTS) professor of marketing Natalina Zlatevska said shoppers often rely on the labels as a shortcut when making quick decisions in supermarkets.

items.title: Teleskop pada na Zemlju. NASA pokreće prvu spasilačku misiju
items.summary: LANSIRANJE u subotu bit će početak nove ere.

items.title: Oldest example of preserved tube feet reveals clues about the lives of 452-million-year-old sea lilies
items.summary: Echinoderms, such as starfish, sea urchins and sea lilies, use small, flexible, tubular projections called "tube feet" for locomotion, feeding, respiration and sensory perception. Crinoids, a subgroup of echinoderms, are known to have a long fossil record, but these fossils usually preserve only har

items.title: Early humans were bringing fire into caves 1.8 million years ago
items.summary: A new study suggests early humans were using fire in South Africa’s Wonderwerk Cave as far back as 1.79 million years ago. Researchers found burned bones deep inside the cave, where natural wildfires could not have reached, indicating that fire was likely carried in and maintained by human ancestors

items.title: Heat waves threaten health of both young and old
items.summary: A brutal heat wave blanketing much of Europe has prompted reminders that extreme heat poses a particular threat to the health of children and the elderly.

items.title: ALMA spots a nine-member stellar family in the act of formation
items.summary: Massive stars much bigger than our sun always come in pairs or groups, not alone. But astronomers don't fully understand how these groupings form. In a new study, astronomers using ALMA have serendipitously discovered a young system containing nine baby stars forming together, and they have det

items.title: H5 bird flu detected in second Australia state
items.summary: Scientists have detected the highly contagious H5 bird flu strain in a second Australian state, the nation's agriculture minister said Wednesday.

items.title: Prva snimka čuda prirode: Pauk od mreže radi oprugu pa lansira mrave brže od F1
items.summary: AUSTRALSKI pauk je priv put snimljen kako gradi genijalnu zamku koja mrave katapultira brže od bolida Formule 1.
